the mathematical structures of physics
e. quantum mechanics II: the quantum particle
introduction
  • e.0. introduction

quantum mechanics II: the quantum particle

Table of contents

  1. The Weyl $\upC^\ast$-algebra

Quantum dynamics

The Weyl $\upC^\ast$-algebra

Theorem/Definition. Let $\calv$ be a symplectic vector space with symplectic form $\ang{-,-}$. For each vector $v\in \calv$, let

\begin{equation}\notag W(v): l^2(\calv) \to l^2(\calv) \end{equation}

be the function defined by

\begin{equation}\notag \left(W(v)(f)\right)(w) = e^{i\ang{w,v}/2} f(v+w), \quad \forall f\in l^2(\calv), \ w\in \calv. \end{equation}

Then:

  1. Each $W(v)$ is a unitary linear operator on $l^2(\calv)$, with inverse \begin{equation}\notag W(v)^\ast = W(-v). \end{equation}

  2. For each $v,w\in \calv$, we have \begin{equation}\notag W(v)W(w) = e^{i\ang{w,v}/2}W(v+w) \end{equation}

  3. For each $v,w\in \calv$, we have \begin{equation}\notag W(v)W(w) = e^{i\ang{v,w}}W(w)W(v). \end{equation}

  4. If $\ang{v,w}=0$, then \begin{equation}\notag W(v)W(w) = W(v+w). \end{equation}

The $\upC^\ast$-subalgebra of $\calb(l^2(\calv))$ generated by all the operators $W(v)$, denoted $\calw(\calv)$, is called the Weyl $\upC^\ast$-algebra generated by $\calv$.
